title: The Twenty-fifth Sunday after Pentecost
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
## Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
## November 10, 2024 | Preacher: Katie Pearson

---

LITURGICAL CONTEXT

The Twenty-fifth Sunday after Pentecost falls in Ordinary Time, the longest liturgical season in the Christian calendar, which extends from Pentecost through the beginning of Advent. Under the Revised Common Lectionary (RCL), this date would feature readings likely including a passage from the Hebrew scriptures, a psalm, an epistle reading (possibly from 1 Thessalonians), and a Gospel reading (likely from Matthew, Mark, or Luke depending on the year's cycle). The proximity to Advent (beginning December 1, 2024) means the lectionary may begin emphasizing eschatological themes about Christ's return and the end times. This is one of the final Sundays before the church year resets with the season of Advent.

EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church in November 2024 was in the aftermath of General Convention (held biennially; the most recent was June 2024 in Louisville), so diocesan leadership would have been implementing new policies around marriage equality, racial justice, and pandemic-related changes to worship protocols. The Diocese of Colorado, which encompasses Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral (located in downtown Denver at 1313 Clarkson Street), would have been engaged in typical autumn programming and stewardship campaigns heading toward year-end. No major bishop elections or crisis-level denominational controversies were occurring in early November 2024, though the Episcopal Church continued navigating ongoing membership decline and pandemic-related structural changes to parish life.

DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver weather in early November 2024 was in transition toward winter, with typical late autumn temperatures in the 40s-50s Fahrenheit. The Colorado 2024 election cycle had just concluded on November 5, with state and local races decided (Colorado re-elected Democratic officials including Governor Jared Polis); post-election political coverage and analysis would have been dominant in local media. The Capitol Hill neighborhood, where Saint John's Cathedral is located, continues as Denver's most densely populated residential area with ongoing development and gentrification conversations; the cathedral itself sits in the heart of this historic, mixed-income urban neighborhood.

NATIONAL NEWS

The week of November 10, 2024 followed the U.S. general election (November 5), with Donald Trump's presidential victory dominating national coverage and reactions across political divides, churches, and civic institutions. Post-election analysis focused on Trump's decisive victory in key swing states, Republican gains in Congress, and immediate policy questions about immigration, economy, and foreign policy. The broader American mood was deeply polarized, with significant portions of the population experiencing anxiety about the incoming administration's direction on reproductive rights, climate policy, and democratic norms—issues directly relevant to many progressive Episcopal congregations.

INTERNATIONAL NEWS

The Ukraine-Russia war continued as a major international crisis, with Russia maintaining territorial gains and Western debate ongoing about the sustainability of aid to Ukraine under a potential Trump administration. The Middle East remained in acute crisis following the October 2023 Hamas attack on Israel and the subsequent Gaza war, with humanitarian concerns mounting and diplomatic efforts to negotiate ceasefires ongoing. Global economic uncertainty persisted around inflation, interest rates, and geopolitical instability, affecting energy markets and international trade.

CULTURAL MOMENT

Post-election America in November 2024 was characterized by deep national anxiety and polarization, with many progressive religious communities (including Episcopalians) grappling with how to respond prophetically to the political moment while maintaining pastoral care for a divided congregation. The general cultural mood was heavy with concern about the future, particularly around democracy, equality, and justice issues—themes that would naturally surface in preaching at a cathedral in a major progressive urban center. Sports culture was in full swing with the NFL mid-season (Denver Broncos were competitive) and the beginning of NBA and NHL seasons, providing some cultural continuity and distraction from political anxieties.
